checks.a_analysis: The output faithfully follows the text of the source document. No fabricated paragraphs or sentences were found. Expected pipeline additions like heading normalization and metadata were observed but do not constitute fabrication.
The output contains one URL (www.ncgicc.org/) which exists in the source. No fabricated URLs, phone numbers, or emails were found. Note: Omitted URLs are handled under check C5.
All pages (1-10) and all major sections (Present, Proceedings, Priorities, Presentation, Committee Reports, etc.) are represented in the output document.
The source document contains no formal tables; it is primarily structured as text with lists.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: There is a recurring defect where URLs within paragraphs are omitted. Specifically, 'www.ncstreams.net' is missing from the Priority #2 section (page 2) and 'www.nconemap.net' is missing from the Orthoimagery Planning section (page 7). In both cases, the text around the URL is preserved, but the URL itself is dropped, resulting in fragments like 'available on the project website at .'. As these are primary reference points for a government GIS document, their omission is significant.
checks.b_evidence_source: available on the project website at www.ncstreams.net . (Page 2); is on the NC OneMap website, www.nconemap.net. (Page 7)
checks.b_evidence_output: available on the project website at (Element 15) ... . (Element 17); is on the NC OneMap website, (Element 60) ... . (Element 62)

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: Aside from the omitted URLs flagged in C5, the text content is accurate. Minor OCR corrections (e.g., 'objectives' instead of 'ob jectives') and typography normalization (e.g., 'three-tier') are within acceptable limits.

checks.a_analysis: The conversion was audited for duplicated text blocks, particularly across page boundaries and headers/footers. The pipeline successfully excluded the repeating page footers (e.g., 'GICC Minutes, August 8, 2007—1'). No paragraph or section duplications were found.

The heading hierarchy is largely consistent, correctly identifying major sections (H2) and subsections (H3). However, on page 9, the pipeline incorrectly promoted entire content paragraphs into Heading 2 elements for the sections 'Federal Interagency Committee (FIC)', 'GIS Technical Advisory Committee (GIS TAC)', and the first paragraph of 'Management and Operations Committee (M&O)'. In the source, these are bolded paragraph starters (inline headings). By including the full descriptive text of the paragraph within the header tag, the structural navigation for screen readers is corrupted, as body content is incorrectly identified as a heading.
checks.b_evidence_source: Federal Interagency Committee (FIC). Mary Combs reported on the NAIP photography (leaf-on) options for 2008 and the fact that North Carolina will not be flown in 2007. The FIC will continue to discuss land use planning around military installations in North Carolina as directed by the GICC.
checks.b_evidence_location: Federal Interagency Committee (FIC) section, page 9, element 74

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no structural corruption of tables is possible.

The reading order follows the source document top-to-bottom across all ten pages. Paragraphs that span page breaks (e.g., GIS Study on pages 2-3, SAS Corporation mention on pages 3-4) are correctly sequenced in the JSON structure.

The numbered lists for data sharing recommendations and M&O recommendations are correctly identified and preserved as lists in the JSON output. Although the lists are split across page boundaries (e.g., elements 40 and 42 for the nine recommendations), the sequence and meaning are maintained. Promoting 'Action 1' and 'Action 2' into single-item lists is an acceptable reformatting choice.

The document contains several government URLs. The conversion failed to capture the text of two significant URLs: 'www.ncstreams.net' on page 2 and 'www.nconemap.net' on page 7. In both cases, the output text breaks exactly where the URL should appear, resulting in missing information and non-functional links.
checks.b_evidence_source: ...available on the project website at www.ncstreams.net . The Upper French Broad...; ...is on the NC OneMap website, www.nconemap.net. Mr. Nagy said...
checks.b_evidence_output: ...available on the project website at " (order 15) ". The Upper French Broad..." (order 17); "...is on the NC OneMap website, " (order 60) ". Mr. Nagy said..." (order 62)

checks.a_analysis: The HTML rendering faithfully follows the visual organization of the source PDF. The document structure consists of a single-column layout with centered and left-aligned headings, which is preserved in the HTML. The conversion includes a Table of Contents sidebar for navigation, and the content flow matches the source document's progression through meeting minutes, priority updates, and committee reports. All major sections (PRESENT, PROCEEDINGS, Priority items, Study reports, etc.) appear in their correct relative positions.

The source PDF does not contain any data tables. The document is composed entirely of text paragraphs, headings, and numbered/bulleted lists. Consequently, there are no tables to evaluate for rendering defects.

The source PDF is a text-based document of meeting minutes and does not contain any meaningful images such as logos, photos, charts, or diagrams. No image elements were expected in the output, and none are missing or broken.

There is no visual duplication of body content in the HTML output. Paragraphs, action items, and recommendations appear only once in the document flow. While headings appear in both the Table of Contents and the body of the document, this is an intentional accessible design feature and is not considered content duplication. The original page footers (e.g., 'GICC Minutes, August 8, 2007—1') are included in the flow as markers, which accurately reflects their presence in the source material.
